title: Oil Posts 12% Weekly Surge as Structural Supply Shock Deepens, Asia Scrambles for Non-Gulf Routes, and Iraq Races to Build Pipeline Bypasses
subhead: Brent crude hit its biggest weekly gain since April as the collapsed Iran ceasefire choked off tanker traffic, Jeff Currie declared the 'illusion of abundance' over, and China and South Korea pivoted hard away from Hormuz-dependent supply — but a new threat looms at Bab el-Mandeb.

sections.heading: Oil Surges 12% in a Week as Ceasefire Collapse Chokes Middle East Flows
sections.body: Oil prices posted their biggest weekly gain since April, with Brent climbing 12% to around $85 per barrel and WTI reaching $79.88, after the U.S.-Iran ceasefire all but collapsed. The U.S. military struck Iranian targets for six consecutive nights, while Iran hit two UAE-managed oil supertankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz near Oman. The U.S. reinstated its naval blockade outside Hormuz, redirecting three commercial vessels, disabling one Iran-linked tanker near Kharg Island, and boarding another. CENTCOM declared the Strait 'free and open, except for vessels attempting to violate America's steel wall blockade.' Tanker traffic through Hormuz collapsed to a two-month low as operators refused to brave the chokepoint, with the regional threat alert raised to 'severe.'
sections.whyItMatters: The re-escalation has undone weeks of fragile recovery in Middle East oil flows, pushing prompt supply fears back to center stage and flipping Brent futures into backwardation — the market structure signaling immediate scarcity.

sections.heading: Jeff Currie: 'The Illusion of Abundance Is Gone'
sections.body: Carlyle Group Chief Strategy Officer Jeff Currie, formerly Goldman Sachs' head of commodities research, declared that the global oil market's 'illusion of abundance' has vanished, warning of a structural energy shortage rather than a standard supply deficit. Currie pointed to crack spreads hitting an unprecedented $70 per barrel — where the processing spread nearly equals the price of crude itself. He attributed the false perception of plenty to massive inventory drawdowns, including the release of 172 million barrels from the U.S. SPR and 32 IEA member reserves. The IEA separately warned that global oil inventories crashed by over 250 million barrels between March and May, draining at an average rate of 3.8 million bpd, with OECD government stockpiles falling to their lowest since December 1990.
sections.whyItMatters: One of Wall Street's most respected commodities voices is calling this a structural crisis, not a temporary blip. The $70 crack spread and historic inventory depletion mean the global fuel supply cushion is effectively gone heading into peak summer demand.

sections.heading: Asia Pivots Away From Hormuz: China Seeks Non-Gulf LNG, South Korea Routes via Red Sea
sections.body: China's state LNG importers — including PetroChina and Sinopec — are in talks to secure long-term LNG supplies from exporters that don't need the Strait of Hormuz, with Canada emerging as a potential option. China sourced nearly 30% of its LNG from Qatar last year, but Q2 2026 imports from Qatar collapsed to just 100,000 tons, down from 4.7 million tons in the same period a year earlier. Beijing is reluctant to deepen dependence on U.S. LNG amid trade uncertainties under the Trump administration. Meanwhile, South Korea's Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries announced its 14th successful crude shipment via the Red Sea route, loading at Saudi Arabia's Yanbu port. South Korea previously relied on Hormuz for 61% of crude imports and 54% of naphtha imports, and the government called the Red Sea route 'the most realistic option for the time being.'
sections.whyItMatters: Asia's two largest energy importers are actively rewiring supply chains away from the Persian Gulf. The pivot is structural, not temporary — and it's reshaping global trade flows even before the Bab el-Mandeb threat materializes.

sections.heading: Bab el-Mandeb in the Crosshairs as Iran Signals Houthis
sections.body: Iran has instructed Yemen's Houthi movement to stand ready to close the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, the vital gateway to the Red Sea, if ordered by the IRGC. The threat directly jeopardizes the alternative route that South Korea and other Asian importers have been using since Hormuz became unreliable. The Bab el-Mandeb Strait carries roughly 9% of global seaborne trade and is the only maritime access point to the Suez Canal from the south. A simultaneous closure of both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb would cut off nearly 30% of the world's oil and LNG shipping from its primary routes. Pakistan is also seeking emergency spot LNG cargoes for July and August after Hormuz disruptions halted shipments from its Qatari term supplier, paying the highest spot LNG price in four years.
sections.whyItMatters: The Houthi readiness to close Bab el-Mandeb threatens the one viable bypass route that Asian importers have been building around Hormuz. A dual-chokepoint scenario would be unprecedented in modern energy history.

sections.heading: Iraq Becomes the Pipeline Battleground: BP, ConocoPhillips, and Chevron Race In
sections.body: ConocoPhillips agreed to acquire a 42% stake in BP's Kirkuk development subsidiary, targeting over 3 billion barrels of recoverable resources across four major oilfields at a cost of approximately $25 billion. The Kirkuk fields currently produce 285,000–330,000 bpd, down from a historical peak of 1 million bpd. Separately, Chevron signed two memoranda of understanding for the West Qurna 2 and Nassiriya fields, with West Qurna 2 currently producing 460,000 bpd after Iraq nationalized the asset from Russia's Lukoil. Both deals align with Iraqi PM Ali al-Zaidi's visit to Houston to court U.S. investment. Critically, Chevron is also part of a consortium evaluating export pipelines to the Mediterranean via Syria, including the long-dormant 800-km Kirkuk–Baniyas pipeline — a direct bypass of the Strait of Hormuz backed by Washington.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq's pivot toward U.S. supermajors is as much about building overland export routes as it is about boosting production. If pipeline bypasses become operational, Iraq could export crude without ever passing through Hormuz — a geopolitical game-changer.

sections.heading: Brent Flips to Backwardation as Global Inventories Hit Historic Lows
sections.body: The Brent futures curve flipped back into backwardation this week, with the September contract trading nearly $9 per barrel above the six-month forward, the largest premium since June 10. The shift from contango — which had briefly appeared after the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding — lasted less than a month before hostilities erased it. The signal is unmistakable: the market now prices immediate supply as significantly scarcer than future delivery. U.S. SPR stockpiles have fallen to roughly 316 million barrels following record drawdowns, while global observed inventories drained at 3.8 million bpd between March and May. The IEA has warned that the world has 'just weeks' to avoid an economic shock from the combined effects of Hormuz disruption, Russian refinery losses, and depleted strategic reserves.
sections.whyItMatters: Backwardation at this scale is the market screaming for barrels now. With SPRs near historic lows and Middle East flows collapsing, there is virtually no cushion left for further disruptions.

sections.heading: India Tightens Fuel Economy to Cut Oil Imports as Libya Pushes Production Higher
sections.body: India proposed new CAFE-III fuel efficiency standards requiring passenger vehicles to cut consumption from 3.996 to 3.327 liters/100 km by 2031–32, with carbon targets tightening from 113 to 76 g/km. Non-compliance penalties range from ₹2,500 to ₹4,500 per gram of excess CO₂/km, and for the first time, flex-fuel and ethanol vehicles get favorable emission calculations. Automakers plan to launch over 15 new EV models. Meanwhile, Libya's NOC declared the Essar oil discovery commercially viable with estimated reserves of 195 million barrels and 5,000 bpd initial production capacity. Libya's output has climbed to 1.4 million bpd, its highest in over a decade, with targets of 1.6 million bpd by year-end. OMV, Repsol, Eni, and QatarEnergy have all signed exploration deals in the country's first licensing round in 17 years.
sections.whyItMatters: India — the world's third-largest oil importer — is structurally reducing demand through regulation, while Libya is the rare OPEC producer actually growing output. Both shifts matter in a market starved for non-Gulf supply.

bottomLine: The collapsed U.S.-Iran ceasefire has sent oil prices surging 12% in a week, flipping Brent into backwardation and draining already-historic-low inventories. Asia is rewiring supply chains away from Hormuz at speed — China toward non-Gulf LNG and South Korea through the Red Sea — but the Bab el-Mandeb threat puts even that bypass at risk. Iraq is emerging as the new strategic battleground where U.S. supermajors are investing billions not just in oilfields, but in overland pipelines to the Mediterranean. Jeff Currie's warning that the 'illusion of abundance is gone' is backed by $70 crack spreads, 250-million-barrel inventory draws, and SPR levels last seen in 1990. The global energy system is running on empty with no geopolitical off-ramp in sight.
sources: OilPrice.com, Bloomberg, Reuters, IEA, U.S. Central Command, South Korea Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries, India Ministry of Power, Libya NOC, GasBuddy, CENTCOM
